questions.question: Explain the three keywords try, catch, and throw in C++ exception handling.
keyPoints: try block contains code that may throw an exception
catch blocks handle specific exceptions thrown in try block
throw keyword creates and throws an exception object
The exception propagates until caught by matching catch block

questions.question: What is stack unwinding in C++? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Stack unwinding is the process of destroying local objects during exception propagation
Destructors of local objects are called as exception propagates
Example: When func1 calls func2, and func2 throws, func2's local objects are destroyed, then control returns to func1
If func1 catches, its local objects are also destroyed properly

questions.question: Explain the standard exception class hierarchy in C++ with at least four derived classes.
keyPoints: std::exception is the base class
std::logic_error (invalid arguments, domain error)
std::runtime_error (range error, overflow)
std::bad_alloc (new fails)
std::bad_cast (invalid dynamic_cast)
std::ios_base::failure (I/O errors)

questions.question: What is the difference between catching exceptions by value and by reference?
keyPoints: Catch by value: creates a copy of the exception object
Catch by reference: no copy, more efficient, avoids object slicing
Slicing: derived class object becomes base class object when caught by value
Best practice: always catch exceptions by reference

questions.question: Explain the noexcept specifier in C++ with an example.
keyPoints: noexcept indicates a function will not throw exceptions
Syntax: void func() noexcept; or void func() noexcept(true);
If a noexcept function throws, std::terminate is called
Used for optimization - compiler can make assumptions
Can be used with boolean expression: void func() noexcept(condition);

questions.question: What are function try blocks in C++? When are they used?
keyPoints: Function try blocks catch exceptions from both initializer list and constructor body
Syntax: try { // constructor body } catch(...) { }
Used in constructors to handle exceptions from member initialization
Can also be used in regular functions
Allows converting exception to different error handling mechanism

questions.question: Explain the three levels of exception safety guarantees.
keyPoints: Basic guarantee: no resources leaked, objects valid but unknown state
Strong guarantee: either succeeds or throws, state unchanged
Nothrow guarantee: operation will never throw an exception
Example: std::vector::push_back provides strong guarantee

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating exception handling with multiple catch blocks, catch-all handler, and rethrowing of exceptions.
keyPoints: Use try block with multiple statements throwing different types
Implement catch(int), catch(double), catch(const std::exception&) blocks
Include catch(...) as last handler for any unhandled exceptions
Demonstrate rethrowing using throw; in catch block
Show proper order of catch blocks (specific to general)

questions.question: Explain exception handling in constructors and destructors. What special considerations are needed?
keyPoints: Constructors can throw exceptions when initialization fails
When constructor throws, object is not created
Already constructed members have destructors called during unwinding
Destructors should never throw - leads to std::terminate
Use function try blocks for handling initializer list exceptions
Resource acquisition is initialization (RAII) pattern recommended

questions.question: Create a custom exception class in C++ that inherits from std::exception and demonstrate its use in a program.
keyPoints: Create class inheriting from std::exception
Override what() method to return descriptive message
Include appropriate constructors
Throw custom exception in try block
Catch using base class std::exception& or custom class
Demonstrate proper exception handling flow

questions.question: Explain the purpose of exception specifications and how they differ from noexcept. Discuss why exception specifications were deprecated.
keyPoints: Exception specifications: throw(type1, type2) list allowed exceptions
Deprecated because runtime checks were ineffective and caused performance issues
noexcept provides compile-time guarantee
noexcept can be used in templates for conditional compilation
throw() replaced with noexcept in modern C++
Dynamic exception specifications removed from C++17
